Introduction to Public Health
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

02
01
Describe the purpose of public health .
Define and describe terminology and concepts of epidemiology.
03
Identify types of data sources.
04
Identify basic methods of data collection and interpretation.
05
Describe a public health problem in terms of time, place, and person.
Learning Ojectives
06
Identify the key components of a descriptive epidemiology outbreak investigation.

History of Public Health
Sanitation and Environmental Health
Pandemics
Disaster Response and Preparedness
Prevention and Policy

500 BC
Ancient Greek and Romans actively practiced community sanitation measures.
Aqueducts became influence for modern sanitation systems.
Sanitation and Environmental Health
1840s
Public Health Act of 1848 established in United States.
Provided central board of health and placed responsibilities for sanitation in hands of boroughs.
1970
Nixon Administration established the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), which protects human health by safeguarding air, water, and land.

Influenza
Has caused pandemics many times during history.
Preparing for and controlling the effects of influenza remain top priorities for public health.
Pandemics
Polio
Polio was once a common and highly feared disease.
Vaccine introduced in 1955, people lined up.
Eradicated in U.S., only seen in a few countries.
HIV
Emerged during 1980s, became global pandemic.
Diagnostic and treatment interventions have reduced new HIV infections.

Biological warfare
Biological warfare has been used to infect people and animals for centuries.
Public health efforts to monitor and respond to biological warfare are ever ongoing actions.
Preparedness for Disaster Response
September 2001
Public health workers at World Trade Center and Pentagon after 9/11 attack, conducting surveillance for outbreaks of disease or health concerns.
Hurricane Katrina
Public health workers and disaster-relief agencies provided emergency services.
Surveillance for illness and injury among evacuees and refugees.

Book of Leviticus
Third book of Hebrew Bible believed to be first written health code.
Includes personal and community needs for cleanliness, sexual health behaviors, contagious disease, and isolation .
Prevention Through Policy
Tobacco Laws
2000-2024 – cigarette smoking caused $193 billion in annual health-related economic loss.
Laws banning smoking in workplace and other public places have encouraged smokers to quit.
Obesity
Food labeling regulations to highlight fat, sugar, and salt.
Promotion of physical activity in schools, workplace, and neighborhoods.
A Broad Look at Public Health

Public Health problems are diverse
Infectious diseases
Injuries
Chronic diseases
Emergencies
Environmental health problems
Other health threats
Approaching a Public Health Problem
Core Sciences
An Introduction to Epidemiology

The study of the distribution and determinants of health-related states among specific populations and the application of that study to the control of health problems
Purpose of epidemiology
Epidemiology
Discover the agent, host, and environmental factors that affect health
Determine the relative importance of causes of illness, disability, and death
Identify segments of population that have the greatest risk for the specific cause of illness
Evaluate the effectiveness of health programs and services to improve population health
Scientific Approach to Health Problems

Key Terms in Epidemiology
Rate : number of cases occurring during a specific period – dependent on the size of the population during the period
Cluster : group of cases in a specific time and place that might be more than expected
Present among a population at all times
Occurrence in excess of what is expected
Spreads across regions

Calculating Rates
Cannot simply compare number of cases
Let’s say we see 100 cases of foodborne illness in South Bend during one week in August
20 cases at a local restaurant
80 cases in the community
150 people ate at the restaurant
140,000 residents of South Bend

Calculating Rates
Calculating rate will give us better idea of situation
To calculate rate, we need to know
The number of cases of the illness or condition we are counting
The size of the population at risk
The period during which we are calculating the rate

Calculating Rates
20 cases at a local restaurant
80 cases in the community
150 people ate at the restaurant
140,000 residents of South Bend
20 / 150
80 / 140,000
13%
0.05%
A Real Epidemiology Investigation

An Unexplained Pneumonia
In 1976, an unexplained pneumonia was seen in a population of individuals who attended an American Legion Convention.
Hotel Residents – July 21-24, 1976
Hotel Residents – July 21-24, 1976
Hotel Residents – July 21-24, 1976

Identified in January 1977
Legionella pneumophila
Legionnaires’ Diease
Epidemiology Approach and Methods
Types of Epidemiology Studies

Descriptive vs Analytical Epidemiology
Descriptive epidemiology often leads to analytical epidemiology to answer the question… why?
Data Sources

Tractor-associated Fatalities
In 1982, an epidemiologist in the Georgia Department of Public Health became interested in the number of deaths associated with farm tractors
He determined he could examine this problem by using readily available data
D eath certificate records that were included in an existing surveillance system.
He obtained the death certificate records for all deaths that had occurred in Georgia during 1971 through 1981 that were associated with farm tractor incidents.

Descriptive Epidemiology
166 farm tractor-associated deaths
When was the population effected?

Descriptive Epidemiology
166 farm tractor-associated deaths
Who was the population effected?

Descriptive Epidemiology
Where was the population effected?
An Epidemiology Investigation of Legionnaires’ Disease

Background
After such a substantial number of American Legion Conference attendees with similar symptoms were admitted to hospital emergency departments or examined in doctors’ offices, an initial investigation began.

Step 1: establish the existence
Epidemiologists were able to establish the existence of the Legionnaires’ disease outbreak by reviewing records and data
Doctors’ reports, hospital reports, lab results, public health lab confirmation tests, etc.
Thought at the time to be just a pneumonia
Cases w ere indeed higher than normal from the previous weeks or months.

Step 2: prepare for fieldwork
I nvolved preparing for field work by:
researching the outbreak,
gathering necessary supplies and equipment, and
making travel arrangements.
The epidemiologists also consulted with other entities, such as health care workers and the local health department, to determine everyone’s role in the investigation and to learn who the local contacts would be after arriving on the scene.

Step 3: verify diagnosis
T he epidemiologists ensured that the problem was accurately diagnosed by speaking with patients and reviewing laboratory findings and clinical test results.

Step 4: defining and identifying cases
Case definition established to determine if a person “fits”
Four components typically are included in a case definition, including
Clinical information about the disease — What signs and symptoms have been observed?
Characteristics about the persons who are affected — Do any commonalities exist among those who have been ill?
Information about the location or place — Where are the affected persons located?
Specification of time during which the illness onset occurred — What date or time did the illness begin to occur, and what was the symptom duration?

Step 5: using descriptive epidemiology
involved using descriptive epidemiology to describe Legionnaires’ disease and orient the data by identifying:
what,
who,
where,
when,
why, and
how.
The epidemiologist proceeded to study the:
dates,
times,
places, and
persons.:
Number of Legionnaires’ Disease Cases
Rates of Illness Among Delegates

Step 6: develop hypothesis
A hypothesis is an educated guess of how and why an event occurred (i.e. the legionnaires’ outbreak)
Statists are used to test the hypothesis – doesn’t always mean that it will be right
One hypothesis stated that the illness was associated with convention attendees who were guests at or had visited one particular hotel during their time at the convention .

Step 8: refining hypothesis
Not necessary!
Five months after the first cases of Legionnaires’ disease occurred, results of the case-control study indicated that spending time in the lobby of Hotel A was a risk factor for illness.
In December 1976, a CDC laboratorian successfully located the source bacteria after continuing to test the specimens that were thought to be dead.
Investigators concluded that those who had become ill in Philadelphia had inhaled air from the air-conditioning unit at Hotel A, either as guests or while walking on the sidewalk outside the hotel.

Step 9: implementing controls
To minimize growth of the bacteria that causes Legionnaires’ disease, controls were implemented, such as chlorination of water and testing of industrial air-handling systems.

Step 10: communicate findings
I nvestigation findings were communicated to
local health authorities,
the medical community,
the general public, and
lawmakers and other leaders.
During the briefing, epidemiologists describe
what they did and how they did it,
what they learned, and
what should be done about the illness.
